gift_note: A thoughtful gift that feels useful without becoming a lecture — warm friendship-conflict stories beat another feelings chart.
ownership_note: Borrow first on Pip & Posy and Food Group staples — many nurseries already own them. Buy the one your child asks for twice after a messy playdate.
educational_objective: Friend play is messy but words help — stories about friends, sharing, feeling left out, saying sorry and turn-taking. Not basic emotion labelling (28–30m) or my-version worry retelling (31–33m).
age_stage_nuance: At nearly three, parallel play is giving way to friend preferences, toy rows and three-way jealousy. Books should model social repair and turn-taking rules, not name emotions in isolation.
what_to_look_for: Concrete friend-play plots; sharing, turn-taking, sorry and left-out themes; read-aloud length under ten minutes; UK paperback availability; warmth without lecturing.
what_to_avoid: Colour Monster as a fresh buy; KS2 poetry; abstract metaphor; separation-anxiety-only titles; duplicate Big Bright Feelings worry books from 31–33m.
methodology: Benchmark sweep across Mother & Baby sharing guides, Nosy Crow, Scholastic UK, Little Tiger, Hachette and LoveReading4Kids for friendship-conflict and turn-taking picture books (not emotion-labelling titles). Captured structured age ranges from publisher and Scholastic shelf marks — never bare Waterstones interest-age fields. Ranked by fit to three-way jealousy and social repair at nearly-three, borrow-first judgement on household classics, and UK review depth. Ran stage3-url-preflight on 2–3 candidate URLs per title before locking Top 5; set primaries to URLs that passed HTTP smoke and availability (buyable today) on 2026-07-22.
buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order here. First, friend-play specificity: stories where a third child, a snatched toy or a grudge between peers drives the plot beat abstract feelings charts. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a structured publisher or Scholastic age range spanning 34–36 months — not a bare “from 3 years” mark. Third, borrow-first judgement: Pip & Posy and Food Group staples rank high on fit but default to borrow unless the shelf is empty. Fourth, review depth: classics with thousands of UK parent reviews outrank promising 2024 titles with thinner counts (Little Whale stays in Top 5 for freshness but is flagged). Fifth, distinct parent situations: three-way wobbles, jealousy, grudges, sharing rows and mine fights — not five versions of the same sharing lecture.

top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: Two box-play best friends bristle when Shu wants to join their cardboard adventures on Sudden Hill.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Friend play is getting messier now — a third child can feel like being pushed out. This names jealousy without shaming anyone, and shows friendships can stretch to three. Borrow from the library first; it turns up in nurseries a lot.
top_picks.why_it_fits: Models three-way friendship tension, exclusion and repair — the core 34–36m shift beyond emotion labelling.
top_picks.caveats: LoveReading lists “5+ readers” as a reading level — Scholastic’s 3–6 shelf mark is the age signal used here.
top_picks.gift_note: Warm after a messy playdate — not a lecture.
top_picks.ownership_note: Borrow first — common in nurseries and hand-me-down boxes.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Strong parent praise for jealousy-and-repair theme; Bookshop.org aggregate corroborates high scores.
top_picks.preloved_signal_note: Picture books hold up well second-hand.
top_picks.substitute_if_unavailable: Pip and Posy: The New Friend
top_picks.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: three-way jealousy and repair is the hardest nearly-three friend moment — this story centres that exact triangle rather than a two-child beach snub.
top_picks.evidence_notes: Primary set to LoveReading4Kids after Scholastic UK showed out-of-stock on check date and Bookshop.org returned intermittent 403 on availability fetch; Scholastic age mark captured separately.

top_picks.product_name: Pip and Posy: The New Friend
top_picks.age_mark_on_listing: Nosy Crow series age band: Ages 2–5

age_signals.raw_text: Nosy Crow series age band: Ages 2–5

top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: At the beach, Posy wakes to find Pip playing with new friend Zac — jealousy, then a small kind act reunites them.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Not another feelings chart — this is the moment your child watches their best friend laugh with someone else. Posy fixes it herself, which gives you language for real playgroup wobbles. Borrow if you already own other Pip & Posy titles.
top_picks.why_it_fits: Shows jealousy, inclusion and repair in peer play without adult rescue — distinct from 31–33m worry-retelling books.
top_picks.caveats: Paperback is the value pick; board book ISBN differs.
top_picks.gift_note: Warm story gift after a playdate bust-up.
top_picks.ownership_note: High duplication risk — check the shelf and nursery bag first.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Consistently high scores across Nosy Crow editions.
top_picks.preloved_signal_note: Ubiquitous second-hand — check for creased covers only.
top_picks.rank_rationale: #2 over #3: the two-child jealousy beat is more immediately recognisable at playgroup age than Sour Grape’s grudge-loop humour, and Pip & Posy has deeper UK parent recognition.
top_picks.evidence_notes: Primary switched from Nosy Crow to Amazon UK after Nosy Crow returned 403 on availability fetch while remaining smoke-OK; Nosy Crow retained as alternate.

top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: A grape who stockpiles grudges learns forgiveness after realising a birthday mix-up — and how sour holding on feels.
top_picks.ember_verdict: When friend play goes wrong, nearly-threes can stay cross long after the toy is back. This is funny enough for repeat reads and gives you a light way into sorry and moving on. Library copy is fine if you already own The Good Egg.
top_picks.why_it_fits: Forgiveness and fairness in friendships — social repair, not basic emotion naming.
top_picks.caveats: Some wordplay lands above toddler level; the visual story carries it.
top_picks.gift_note: Funny enough to re-read after a squabble.
top_picks.ownership_note: Food Group series is widely owned — borrow before buying another.
top_picks.rating_source: Amazon UK / Goodreads aggregate (UK paperback ISBN 9780063283800)
top_picks.review_quality_note: Scholastic shop shows zero reviews; aggregate drawn from major UK retailers — founder spot-check price.
top_picks.preloved_signal_note: Food Group titles circulate fast second-hand.
top_picks.rank_rationale: #3 over #4: forgiveness after staying cross is a distinct friend-play skill from sharing hardware — and Sour Grape’s review depth beats the newer Little Whale.
top_picks.evidence_notes: Primary set to Scholastic UK (buyable, in stock on check date) after HarperCollins UK returned 429 on preflight.

top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: Little Whale hates sharing the pod, snacks and Mum’s attention until friend Blue models what sharing looks like.
top_picks.ember_verdict: Fresh 2024 pick for the tug-of-war over the good toy or the front of the queue. Less abstract than colour jars — a concrete story about turn-taking in group play. Worth buying if your library hold queue is long.
top_picks.why_it_fits: Sharing and attention jealousy in peer contexts — matches turn-taking cluster without repeating 28–30m emotion-label books.
top_picks.caveats: Newer title — fewer reviews than classics; strong editorial comparables.
top_picks.gift_note: Good gift if they do not already own three sharing books.
top_picks.ownership_note: Lower duplication risk than Pip & Posy — fewer households own it yet.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Review count above bar but thinner than classics — flagged for founder spot-check.
top_picks.rank_rationale: #4 over #5: group sharing and attention jealousy is a sharper nearly-three turn-taking lesson than a two-character chair fight, and it is the freshest title parents are less likely to already own.
top_picks.evidence_notes: Age range taken from Penguin Random House publisher data for the title family; UK Little Tiger edition ISBN 9781801044073 confirmed in stock on check date.

top_picks.product_description_under_30_words: Two furry friends battle over one chair, shouting “It’s mine!” until they choose to play together instead.
top_picks.ember_verdict: The “that’s MINE” phase meets friend play. Short, shouty text mirrors real arguments, then pivots to shared play — handy after playdates where nobody wanted to swap. Borrow if you already have two sharing books on the go.
top_picks.why_it_fits: Turn-taking and territory fights between peers — practical social rules, not naming happy/sad.
top_picks.caveats: Hachette product page highlights ebook ISBN; paperback edition cross-checked via publisher age data.
top_picks.gift_note: Funny enough to gift to a family without a big picture-book shelf.
top_picks.ownership_note: Borrow-first — overlaps with other sharing titles many nurseries already stock.
top_picks.review_quality_note: Solid UK review base across paperback editions.
top_picks.preloved_signal_note: Picture books hold up well second-hand.
top_picks.substitute_if_unavailable: Pip and Posy: The Super Scooter
top_picks.rank_rationale: #5: the chair fight is the quickest read for mine-shouting rows — kept in Top 5 as the low-friction borrow-first option when you need language tonight, not another fresh purchase.
top_picks.evidence_notes: Age range uses Publishers Weekly / Common Sense “ages 2–7” band — not Hachette’s bare “Interest age: From 3 years” field, which fails 34–36m hygiene.

longlist.summary_reason: Playdate sharing gone wrong — Stuffie tug-of-war, torn toy, Mama helps repair.
longlist.rank_rationale: #6 over #7: a more direct playdate-sharing bust-up than Squirrels’ adventure metaphor.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Slightly more adult-led repair at the end — Top 5 picks keep peer-to-peer jealousy and repair centre stage.

longlist.summary_reason: Two greedy squirrels race for the last nut until teamwork saves them.
longlist.rank_rationale: #7 over #8: competitive sharing via adventure beats apology-only plots for repeat read-aloud energy.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Animal adventure metaphor is looser on real friend-play jealousy than On Sudden Hill or Pip & Posy.

longlist.summary_reason: Kite accident sparks 'HORRIBLE BEAR!' — mutual mistakes lead to sorry.
longlist.rank_rationale: #8 over #9: apology after a friend fallout is a distinct backup to grudge-loop Top Pick #3.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Accident-plus-sorry arc is narrower than three-way jealousy or group sharing in the Top 5.

longlist.product_name: Pip and Posy: The Super Scooter
longlist.age_mark_on_listing: Nosy Crow series age band: Ages 2–5
longlist.summary_reason: Posy snatches Pip's scooter, falls, apologises — plaster and sandcastle reset.
longlist.rank_rationale: #9 over #10: snatch-apologise-repair in one Pip & Posy arc is the tightest series backup.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Two-child scooter row is less specific to three-way friend wobbles than The New Friend.

longlist.summary_reason: Little Nutbrown Hare meets Tipps and asks the big friendship question.
longlist.rank_rationale: #10: gentle first-friendship nerves round out the backup bench after snatch-and-sorry picks.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: First-friendship nerves are softer than jealousy, sharing rows or grudges — better as a library borrow.

longlist.product_name: Sometimes I'm a Baby Bear, Sometimes I'm a Snail
longlist.summary_reason: Animal comparisons help children say when they want to play vs need quiet.
longlist.rank_rationale: #11: boundary language is a useful backup when friend play gets overwhelming.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Boundary/space theme is adjacent — not a sharing or jealousy plot.

longlist.summary_reason: Tiny Hugasaurus learns hugs don't fix every playground row — kindness with limits.
longlist.rank_rationale: #12: kindness-with-limits is a softer backup after sharper conflict picks.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Kindness reset is less specific than Top 5 sharing or jealousy stories.

longlist.summary_reason: Meesha makes her own friends from craft bits when joining in feels hard.
longlist.rank_rationale: #13: shy-joiner craft friends suit nursery transitions more than home playdate rows.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Joining-in anxiety is slightly older than nearly-three mine fights and three-way jealousy.

longlist.summary_reason: Three frogs squabble over a pond until a storm forces sharing.
longlist.rank_rationale: #14: classic three-way sharing metaphor — strong but well known.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: Classic fable format is less immediate than contemporary friend-play stories in Top 5.

longlist.product_name: I'm Going to Make a Friend
longlist.summary_reason: A child builds an imaginary friend, then a real one joins — vulnerability and courage.
longlist.rank_rationale: #15: new-setting friendship courage is a niche backup for moves or nursery starts.
longlist.missed_top5_reason: School/move themes sit slightly above the everyday playdate brief.

skips.skip_reason: Basic colour-coded emotion labelling — right for 28–30m, not friendship conflict at nearly three. Bring back out if you already own it.
Feelings vocabulary book — useful from the shelf, but not the friend-play and turn-taking brief for 34–36m.
Parent–child separation comfort — not peer friendship, sharing or turn-taking.
General mood tools — weak on friend-play conflict; better as solo feelings book.
Worry/anxiety focus — useful but not the friendship-and-turn-taking brief.
Beautiful sadness metaphor — no sharing, sorry or friend-play arc.
31–33m band book — 'my version' worry retelling, not social-rules friend play.

guidance_notes.title: Borrow before you buy
guidance_notes.body: Nearly-threes churn through picture books fast. Library holds on Pip & Posy, Food Group and Rachel Bright titles cover most playdate rows — buy the one your child asks for twice.

guidance_notes.title: Bring back out
guidance_notes.body: If you already own 28–30m feelings labellers (Colour Monster, In My Heart), bring them back for vocabulary — but add one friendship-conflict story from this list rather than another emotion chart.

qa_summary.notes: Full Mode A re-run 2026-07-22 with publisher/Scholastic primaries and structured age_signals.
